title: Lesson 223
transcript: What can companies acquire? All sorts of things, hardware, software, services, even people. But let's focus on services because nowadays companies typically acquire all sorts of different services to support their business. And security has a very important function to play in the acquisition of services. And that is making sure that these new services that are being acquired are actually secure. So let's think about where should security be involved in the procurement process of new services? When should security become involved in this process? And the answer is at the very earliest stages. Because when you think about procuring services, the first thing that's required is to actually first understand the business requirements. What does the business actually want to do with the services? What does the service actually have to provide? And it's at this earliest stage that security should be involved because you need to really clearly understand exactly what the service is and what it's going to do for the organization and therefore what the security implications are. For instance, if the service that you're gonna be acquiring is gonna be processing PCI data, payment card industry credit card data, that suddenly brings with it a lot of additional security implications or say it's healthcare data or personal information. These different types of data, these different services that are going to be required have very different security requirements. So the place that security starts with acquiring any services is to first understand the business requirements, understand precisely what the business is going to be doing with the system, with the service, and then making sure that all the correct security requirements have been defined. And we often refer to these as the minimum security requirements. What are the minimum security that needs to be provided by the service to meet the business's needs? These security requirements get documented in a document called the SLR Service Level Requirements. This is a document that will list out what are all the different security requirements that need to be provided by the service. And this SLR document will actually be used as part of the procurement process. So the procurement team that is going out soliciting bids and proposals by different service providers and then evaluating all of those different services will be using this SLR document as part of the procurement process to make sure or to evaluate the different service providers against the requirements listed in the SLR and help the organization choose a particular service offering that's going to meet all the defined security requirements. And in the next module, we'll talk about when an organization starts actually acquiring a service. How do these SLR requirements get translated into another very important document? the SLA.
